title: Data Update 3 for 2026: The Trust Deficit - Bonds, Currencies, Gold and Bitcoin!
gist: Damodaran argues that four 2025 shocks (tariffs, the Moody's downgrade, the record government shutdown, and Fed-independence attacks) eroded institutional trust, but the damage showed up very unevenly across markets: bonds and equities shrugged it off, the dollar weakened moderately, gold and silver went vertical, and bitcoin behaved like a risk asset rather than a trust hedge.

insights.claim: A loss of trust in institutions (governments, central banks, regulators) is the single thread that best explains the divergent 2025 performance of treasuries, currencies, gold/silver, and bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should read cross-asset divergence in 2026 as a trust barometer, not just a rates/inflation story — watch gold/silver and the dollar as the most sensitive gauges.
insights.quote: a loss of trust in institutions (governments, central banks, regulatory authorities) was the thread that best explains their performance
insights.locator: In this one, I want to focus specifically on four news stories

insights.claim: The traditional developed-vs-emerging-market trust gap has narrowed in the 21st century because crises and political developments have undercut trust in institutions across the board, including in developed markets like the US.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The old playbook of treating US/developed-market sovereign risk as categorically different from emerging-market risk needs revisiting; risk premia gaps between the two may keep compressing.
insights.locator: one of the divides between developed and emerging markets

insights.claim: The 2025 trust shocks (tariffs, ratings downgrade, Fed independence fight) are not new phenomena caused solely by Donald Trump but are the culmination of much longer-running trends: trade backlash a decade-plus in the making, prior downgrades by S&P (2011) and Fitch (2023), and Fed independence that was always norm-based rather than legally entrenched.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors shouldn't expect a single political change to reverse the trust erosion; it's structural and likely to persist across administrations.
insights.quote: the truth is that in each case, the slippage has been occurring over much longer
insights.locator: While Donald Trump is the easy answer

themes.theme: Bond Market: Muted Reaction Despite the Shocks

insights.claim: Despite the Moody's downgrade, the record government shutdown, and tariff turmoil, the 10-year US treasury rate actually fell 39 basis points over 2025, from 4.58% to 4.19%, with short-term treasuries falling even more.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The bond market did not price a default/trust crisis in 2025 — rates fell rather than rose, contradicting the intuitive 'trust deficit means higher yields' narrative.
insights.quote: the ten-year treasury rate dropped by 39 basis points (from 4.58% to 4.19%) during the year
insights.locator: US Treasury Data

insights.claim: The yield curve steepened materially in 2025: the 10-year/30-year spread doubled, the 10-year/2-year spread widened by 7 basis points, while the 2-year/3-month spread narrowed, leaving a much more upward-sloping curve by year-end.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A steepening curve is a marginal positive signal for future US growth, though Damodaran cautions against over-reading the yield curve as a recession predictor.
insights.locator: During 2025, the spread between the 10-year and 30-year treasury doubled

insights.claim: US sovereign CDS spreads spiked from about 0.4% to just over 0.5% in April 2025 around the tariff shock, but had fully round-tripped back below start-of-year levels by year end.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Default-risk pricing shows the tariff shock, not the downgrade or shutdown, was the real market-moving event, and even that was fully reversed within the year.
insights.locator: sovereign CDS spreads for the US

insights.claim: Market-implied inflation expectations (10-year treasury minus 10-year TIPS) barely moved in 2025 and ended the year lower than they started, contradicting expert forecasts of an inflation surge after the tariff announcements but matching actual reported inflation.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The market was a better inflation forecaster than the expert consensus on tariff-driven inflation fears in 2025 — a data point for weighting market-based inflation signals over survey/expert forecasts going forward.
insights.quote: That would have put the market at odds with experts, who forecasted a surge in inflation especially after the tariffs were announced
insights.locator: To get a sense of how expectation of inflation changed

insights.claim: Corporate bond spreads diverged sharply by quality in 2025: investment-grade (BBB and above) spreads were flat to lower, while below-investment-grade (sub-BBB) spreads widened.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Credit risk repricing was concentrated in junk-rated issuers, a sign that any stress from the trust deficit is being absorbed at the lower end of credit quality, not broadly.
insights.locator: divergence in how the year played out in the corporate bond market

themes.theme: Fed Independence and the Rate-Setting Framework

insights.claim: The Fed's influence over interest rates is vastly overstated: long-term treasury rates are driven mainly by expected inflation and real growth, not by Fed Funds rate decisions, which mainly affect short-term treasuries.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors overweighting Fed meeting outcomes for long-duration positioning may be mispricing the actual drivers — real growth and inflation trajectory matter more for the 10-year+ curve.
insights.quote: I believe that the role of Fed in setting interest rates is vastly overstated
insights.locator: On the final question of why the Fed independence fight

insights.claim: Using an intrinsic 10-year riskfree rate (actual inflation plus real GDP growth) as a benchmark, the treasury rate of 4.18% at the start of 2026 is converging toward the intrinsic rate of 5.10%, closing a gap that persisted from 2021-2025 when the intrinsic rate ran well above the actual treasury rate.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The gap closing suggests treasury rates may still have room to normalize upward toward the intrinsic rate, or inflation/growth could decelerate to meet the treasury rate instead.
insights.locator: the treasury rate of 4.18%, at the start of 2026, is within reach of the intrinsic rate of 5.10%

insights.claim: Treasury rates stayed below the intrinsic risk-free rate during 2021-2025 because markets trusted the Fed would fight inflation even at the cost of a recession; if the Fed is perceived as becoming an extension of the government, that belief could break, risking a self-reinforcing spiral of higher inflation feeding higher rates feeding more inflation.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A credible attack on Fed independence is a tail risk for a stagflationary spiral, not just a governance story — worth monitoring nominee/appointment news as a leading indicator.
insights.quote: creating a cycle where higher inflation causes higher interest rates, and attempts by central banks to lower these rates actually feed into even higher inflation
insights.locator: It is this belief that will be put at risk

insights.claim: It is in politicians' own interest to preserve central bank independence, because it produces better economic outcomes and lower interest rates while giving politicians cover for unpopular but necessary policy choices.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A normative argument for why attacks on Fed independence are self-defeating even from a purely political standpoint — a lens for judging future administration rhetoric.
insights.locator: It is in the best interests of governments and politicians

themes.theme: Currency Market: The Dollar Absorbs the Trust Hit

insights.claim: Unlike bonds, the currency market clearly registered the trust deficit: the trade-weighted dollar fell 7.24% in 2025, losing more value against developed-market currencies (down 8.19%) than against emerging-market currencies (down 6.34%).
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The dollar was the clearest single-asset expression of the 2025 trust deficit, and its underperformance was concentrated versus developed-market peers rather than emerging markets — worth tracking DM vs EM FX divergence as a trust gauge.
insights.quote: it was down 8.19% against the former and 6.34% against the latter
insights.locator: The trade-weighted dollar, a broad index of the dollar

themes.theme: Gold and Silver: The Trust-Deficit Trade

insights.claim: Gold rose 65% in 2025, one of its best years ever, while silver rose an even larger 148%, even though neither the high-unexpected-inflation nor the intense-crisis conditions that historically drive precious-metal rallies were present.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The magnitude of the 2025 gold/silver rally is anomalous versus historical drivers, implying a structural shift (trust-seeking capital) rather than a cyclical inflation or crisis trade — a signal that could persist rather than mean-revert quickly.
insights.quote: Gold had one of its best years ever, rising 65% during the year, and silver... rising 148%
insights.locator: Gold and Silver

insights.claim: The ratio of gold price to CPI broke through historic norms in 2025, invalidating the 'golden rule' heuristic that gold prices roughly track inflation over time.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Standard gold valuation heuristics (gold tracks CPI) failed in 2025; investors relying on that anchor to call gold overvalued would have missed a 65% rally.
insights.quote: gold price performance in 2025 broke the indicator, as the ratio of gold price to the CPI exploded well above historic norms
insights.locator: if you scale gold price to the CPI

insights.claim: Central banks increased gold holdings in 2025 due to loss of trust in the US government and dollar, but this buying, while contributing to gold's allure, cannot by itself explain the magnitude of the price rise.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't over-attribute the gold rally to central-bank reserve diversification alone — private/speculative trust-driven demand was likely a larger driver.
insights.quote: that increase, while contributing to gold's allure, cannot explain its price rise during the year
insights.locator: It is worth noting that a loss of trust

insights.claim: Silver's 2025 surge pushed the gold-to-silver price ratio below its long-term median value.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Silver outperforming gold enough to compress the ratio below its historical median suggests a broader precious-metals mania rather than a narrow flight-to-gold, a distinction relevant for relative-value trades between the two metals.
insights.locator: silver prices rose enough to bring the ratio of gold to silver prices

insights.claim: Markets are pulling in different directions on the trust question: stocks and bonds are largely dismissing the trust deficit, currencies show moderate concern, and gold/silver imply investors see much larger consequences, reflecting a market that is not a monolith but contains a meaningful subset positioning for catastrophic risk.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The precious metals rally may reflect a minority but well-capitalized 'catastrophe insurance' cohort rather than consensus market view — useful context for judging how durable the gold/silver bid is.
insights.quote: there is a significant segment of investors that see catastrophic risks emerging, and piling into precious metals
insights.locator: It seems like the market is pulling in different directions

insights.claim: Bitcoin ended 2025 down 6.4% in US dollar terms and down 17.4% in Euro terms, despite a macro backdrop (weakening dollar, questioned Fed independence) that should have favored a central-bank-free currency/collectible.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Bitcoin failed the 2025 real-world test of behaving as a trust hedge, undercutting the thesis that it benefits mechanically from central-bank and government distrust.
insights.quote: bitcoin ended the year down 6.4% in US dollar terms and 17.4% in Euro terms
insights.locator: The months following have vindicated that view

insights.claim: Bitcoin surged in the middle of 2025, appearing to validate bullish narratives, but then both bitcoin and MicroStrategy suffered pricing collapses after Damodaran's July 2025 argument against companies putting cash balances into bitcoin proved out.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The corporate-treasury-bitcoin strategy (MicroStrategy-style) took a real hit in 2025, a cautionary data point for other companies or investors considering copying that playbook.
insights.locator: In my post on bitcoin on July

insights.claim: Across 2025 weekly returns, bitcoin was much more highly correlated with US equities than with gold or silver, suggesting it draws in risk-seeking capital rather than risk-averse, trust-hedging capital.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Bitcoin should be modeled as a high-beta risk asset correlated with equities for portfolio construction purposes, not as an uncorrelated store-of-value/gold substitute.
insights.quote: bitcoin is much more highly correlated with US equities than it is with its collectible counterparts, suggesting that it draws in risk seekers, not the risk averse
insights.locator: There are only a few co-movements

insights.claim: It remains too early to render final judgment on bitcoin's ultimate role, but the 2025 evidence points to gold and silver, not bitcoin, being the actual destination for trust-deficit capital.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors using bitcoin as a portfolio hedge against institutional/currency distrust should reconsider gold/silver as the empirically better-performing hedge in the actual 2025 stress test.
insights.quote: gold and silver were the draws, at least in 2025, not bitcoin
insights.locator: It remains too early in bitcoin's life

themes.theme: Cross-Asset Correlations and the Fed-Stock Narrative

insights.claim: On a week-to-week basis in 2025, higher interest rates (i.e., negative bond returns) were accompanied by higher stock prices, casting doubt on the notion that US stock markets are being propped up by Fed rate activity or inactivity.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The popular 'Fed put' narrative that equities rally only because of low/falling rates doesn't hold up in the 2025 weekly correlation data — a reason to be skeptical of rate-cut-dependent equity bull theses.
insights.quote: higher interest rates (translating into negative bond returns) were accompanied by higher stock prices, casting doubt on the notion that the stock market is being held afloat by Fed activity or inactivity
insights.locator: The second is that notwithstanding the fact that US treasuries

insights.claim: The overall 2025 conclusion is that the trust deficit did not produce the textbook outcome of rising rates and a bad year for bonds; instead the damage was selectively absorbed by the dollar, and dramatically, by gold and silver, while bitcoin tracked stocks and bonds without their upside.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: For 2026 positioning, treat gold/silver and the dollar as the primary transmission channels for any further trust deterioration, not bond yields.
insights.quote: Bitcoin remained the outlier, moving more with stocks and bonds, albeit without their upside (at least this year) and less with collectibles
insights.locator: Conclusion
